PAGE: Harry Goatleaf
CATEGORIES: Agents of Saruman, Minor characters (The Lord of the Rings)
URL: https://lotr.fandom.com/wiki/Harry_Goatleaf
======================================================================

Harry Goatleaf
Biographical information
Titles
Gate-keeper
Birth
Sometime before
September 23
TA 3018
Death
In or after
TA 3018
Realms
Bree
,
Chetwood
/
wilds north-way
Physical description
Race
Men
Culture
Bree-landers
(
Bree-men
)
Media portrayal
Actor
Martyn Sanderson
Voice
Tom Kane
(
video game
)
Martyn Sanderson (
video game
)
"
I meant no offence. But you’ll find maybe that more folk than old Harry at the gate will be asking you questions. There’s queer folk about. If you go on to
The Pony
, you’ll find you’re not the only guests.
"
—
Harry Goatleaf to
Frodo Baggins
.
Harry Goatleaf
was a
Bree-man
of the
Goatleaf
family who worked as the
Gate-keeper
of the
West-gate
of
Bree
during the time of the
War of the Ring
.
History
Harry Goatleaf lived with
his brother
Ned
in a small, dark gray house close to the
West-gate
of
Bree
.
He worked as a
Gate-keeper
stationed at a lodge next to the western gate, which was located on the great hedge surrounding the township of Bree.
On
September 23
in the year
3018
,
Strider
observed a
Nazgûl
having a conversation with Goatleaf before leaving him white and shaking.
On
September 29
, Goatleaf encountered the
Hobbits
Frodo Baggins
,
Meriadoc Brandybuck
,
Peregrin Took
, and
Samwise Gamgee
when they arrived at Bree. Goatleaf got a lantern and questioned them gruffly about their intentions, silently observing that they came from
the Shire
before letting them in. Before wishing them good night, he warned them that there would be more folk besides him at
The Prancing Pony
who would ask them questions.
When Strider advised the four Hobbits to be more careful as the Nazgûl were seen
passing
the
Greenway
the previous Monday, Frodo realized that he should have known this from the way Goatleaf spoke to them. Later, when Strider predicted that something may happen that night before they leave Bree, he mentioned that Goatleaf may be a spy for the Nazgûl, noting the conversation he had overheard the previous Monday.
Before the end of 3018, the
Bree-men
kicked the
Ruffians
among the
Southerners
out of Bree. Early in the New Year of
3019
however, the town was attacked in the night by the very same Ruffians. During the battle, Goatleaf fought on the side of the Ruffians along with
Bill Ferny
. After the battle, Goatleaf left Bree alongside the other Ruffians, hiding out in either the
Chetwood
beyond
Archet
, or in the wilds north of Bree. Later, it was believed by
Barliman Butterbur
that Goatleaf and Ferny were the ones who let the Ruffians in on the night of the battle.
It is likely that Goatleaf's position as a Gate-keeper was presumably later taken by another Gate-keeper.
In other versions
In an early draft, Harry Goatleaf mentions as he let the hobbits in that he laughed at
some people
who asked to watch for them a few days before.
Later, Goatleaf appears in a corner of the
Common Room
of
The Prancing Pony
where he whispered with Bill Ferny and the
squint-eyed Southerner
and slipped out behind them upon witnessing Frodo's
disappearance
after his performance of a
folk song
.
Later on in the draft, Goatleaf is described as "a mean old curmudgeon" by
Trotter
.
In another draft,
Gandalf
is at the gate and asks Goatleaf if he had seen any
Shire-hobbits
.
Goatleaf revealed that he did not, as he was in
Staddle
and
his brother
was at the gate in his place.
After advising Gandalf to head to the inn, he shut the gate.
Later on in this draft, the Black Riders came to the gate and Harry revealed Gandalf's entry.
A few moments later, when Frodo passed through, he was more friendly than in the published text, and the dialogue was slightly expanded.
After letting the Hobbits in, he tells Ned in his house to take his place as he leaves his post.
In adaptations
Pán prsteňov
In the 2001-3
Slovak radio series
, Harry Goatleaf makes a brief appearance early in the second episode of the radio series. He is portrayed by an uncredited actor, thus making him the only notable character in the entire series to go uncredited.
The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring
In
Peter Jackson
's 2001
film adaptation
, Harry Goatleaf, portrayed by
Martyn Sanderson
, is never named, but is referred to as the
Gate Keeper
in the end credits. Unlike in the novel, he doesn't seem to be in league with the Black Riders. In fact, he is killed when the Black Riders knock the gate down and crush him underneath it as they storm Bree.
The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring
(video game)
In the
2002
video game
, Harry Goatleaf is first encountered as one of the patrons in
The Prancing Pony
, where he warns Frodo that suspicious folk have been seen in Bree, and seemingly shows concern for Frodo's safety and well-being. He is later seen in a cutscene opening the town's gates and letting
wolves
loose into the streets. After this, he is never seen again. He is voiced by an uncredited
Tom Kane
.
LEGO The Lord of the Rings: The Video Game
In portable version of the
2007
LEGO video game
, Harry Goatleaf appears identically to the 2001 film.
Harry Goatleaf in
The Lord of the Rings Online
The Lord of the Rings Online
In the 2007 video game,
The Lord of the Rings Online
, Harry Goatleaf is found at the West-gate of Bree, and for a long time he was not involved in any quests. He later appeared alongside
Bill Ferny
as one of
Lotho Sackville-Baggins
' enforcers in the Shire and participated in an attack on
The Prancing Pony
during the winter of 3019.

PAGE: Herefara

Herefara
Biographical information
Death
March 15
,
3019
Physical description
Race
Men
Culture
Rohirrim
Herefara
was a
Rohirrim
warrior who served
King
Théoden
during the
War of the Ring
. He fought and died in the
Battle of the Pelennor Fields
.
His name was remembered in the
song of the Mounds of Mundburg
.
Etymology
Herefara
is
Old English
for "war-traveler".
In adaptations
In
The Lord of the Rings Online
, Herefara comes from the town of Woodhurst in the Stonedeans, a province in western
Rohan
where many men have a mix of Rohirrim and
Dunlending
ancestry. His grandparents are Briant and Reeve Heremond; and his parents are Cynethryth and
Herubrand
. Under the influence of
Saruman
, in early
March
TA 3019
a conspiracy of men attacks the Reeve's family in the Mead Hall of Woodhurst. Heremond and Cynethryth are slain but Herubrand, who is wounded, is saved by his loyalist who hide him and his mother outside the city. They enlist the player, an outsider who could not have been involved the attack, to help them in uncovering the conspiracy and eventually take back the control of his city. Herefara is not present during these events, as he is leading the defense of Brockbridge, a village ruled by
Háma
's father Léoferth to the south from the forces of
Isengard
. Herubrand and Herefara later meet in the
Drúadan Forest
during the ride of the Rohirrim towards
Minas Tirith
. Herefara joins the player in patrolling the lands around beacons
Nardol
and
Eilenach
. During the
Battle of the Pelennor Fields
the father and son fight side by side but are slain by
Gothmog
's
Orcs
.
References

PAGE: Herubrand

Herubrand
Biographical information
Birth
Late-
Third Age
Death
March 15
,
TA 3019
Spouse
Unnamed wife
Weapon
Sword
Physical description
Race
Men
Culture
Rohirrim
Media portrayal
Actor
Unknown
Herubrand
was a
Rohirrim
warrior who served as a guard of
King
Théoden
. He perished in the
Battle of the Pelennor Fields
, and his name was remembered in the
song of the Mounds of Mundburg
.
Etymology
Herubrand
is
Old English
for "war-sword".
In adaptations
Herubrand in
The Lord of the Rings Online
In
The Lord of the Rings Online
, Herubrand comes from the town of Woodhurst in the Stonedeans, a province in western
Rohan
where many men have a mix of Rohirrim and
Dunlending
ancestry. His parents are Briant and Reeve Heremond; his wife is named Cynethryth and their son is
Herefara
. Under the influence of
Saruman
, in early
March
of
TA 3019
a conspiracy of men attacks the Reeve's family in the Mead Hall of Woodhurst. Heremond and Cynethryth are slain but Herubrand, who is wounded, is saved by his loyalist who hide him and his mother outside the city. Unaware of how many men are involved in the conspiracy, the men loyal to the new Reeve bar the doors of the Mead Hall and keep the attack secret from the people. Herubrand and his advisors enlist the player, an outsider who could not have been involved the attack, to help them in uncovering the conspiracy and eventually take back the control of his city.
Herefara
is not present during these events, as he is leading the defence of the village of Brockbridge to the south from the forces of
Isengard
. Herubrand and Herefara are later met in the
Drúadan Forest
during the ride of Rohirrim towards
Minas Tirith
. During the
Battle of the Pelennor Fields
the father and son fight side by side, but are slain by
Gothmog
's
Orcs
.

PAGE: Hirgon

This article is about the
errand-rider of
Gondor
. For the non-canonical ranger of
Gondor
, see
Hirgon (Ranger)
.
Hirgon
Biographical information
Titles
Errand-rider of
Gondor
Birth
Late
Third Age
Death
March 12
,
TA 3019
Realms
Minas Tirith
,
Gondor
Spouse
Unknown
Physical description
Race
Men
Gender
Male
Height
Tall
Eyes
Grey
Culture
Gondorians
Hirgon
was an emissary of
Minas Tirith
in
Gondor
during the
Great War of the Ring
who presented the
Red Arrow
to
Théoden
,
King of Rohan
.
Characteristics
Hirgon was a grey-eyed tall man who wore a riders' dark green cloak over a fine mail-coat and a helm with "a small silver star" on its front.
When
Meriadoc Brandybuck
first saw him, he thought he was
Boromir
, but then realized that they were just similar in posture and appearance.
Hirgon's companion was "shorter and broader" than him, but dressed like him and looked to Merry to be like Hirgon.
Biography
Hirgon was a mounted messenger that was sent by
Ruling Steward
Denethor II
from
Minas Tirith
to present the
Red Arrow
to
Théoden
, the
King of Rohan
, during the
Great War of the Ring
,
expecting assault from
Sauron
against
Gondor
.
On
March 8
in the year
3019
of the late
Third Age
,
three "Errand-riders of Gondor"
passed
Gandalf
and
Peregrin Took
at about 9:20 pm
in
Anórien
"like flying ghosts in the
moon
" riding westwards to
Rohan
.
On
March 9
,
Hirgon arrived "at night"
with a companion in the camp of the
Rohirrim
at
Dunharrow
.
After explaining to Théoden's guards that he wished to see Théoden, and "the captain of the Guard" relayed the request to the King through the curtain, Hirgon entered the tent without his companion.
He introduced himself as "an errand-rider of Denethor" and presented the Red Arrow to King Théoden while kneeling, explaining that Denethor requested all his forces to come as quickly as possible to Gondor and help defend them.
When Théoden took the Red Arrow and asked what Denethor believed all his strength and speed to be, Hirgon avoided the question before stating that Denethor bid him to tell Théoden that unless he had "the strength to break a siege of many powers", he should have his forces assist the Gondorians on the walls of Minas Tirith.
When asked by Théoden if Denethor knew more than he said in his message, Hirgon said that, though he could not say what "Denethor may know or guess", Gondor was desperate for aid since "many
kings
have ridden in from the
East
to the service of
Mordor
" and "the
Haradrim
are moving" from the
South
.
Théoden promised him that he would set out with six thousand troops for Minas Tirith in the morning, though they would not reach Minas Tirith for a week.
After Hirgon became fearful that the Rohirrim would be too late to save Minas Tirith other than to avenge it, Théoden assured him that, if that happened, they would do that; he then told Hirgon to stay until the next day since the "night changes many thoughts" and counsels are better in the morning.
At "about mid-day on"
March 10
,
after discussing with Théoden the
Dawnless Day
and the necessary provisions for the Rohirrim on their ride, Hirgon and his companion rode back to Gondor.
Sometime around the night of
March 12
, Hirgon and his companion reached the
Rammas Echor
, but could not pass through it to reach Minas Tirith, probably because of
Easterling
forces.
As a consequence, the two turned back westwards to flee when they were killed along with their horses between the Grey Wood and the Rammas Echor.
Though Hirgon's "head was hewn off", his hand still clutched the Red Arrow.
On
March 14
,
when the riders of Rohan reached the
Grey Wood
and camped beneath
Min-Rimmon
there,
Elfhelm
reported to Théoden that some scouts found two dead messengers and two dead horses between the Grey Wood and the Rammas Echor.
He speculated that Hirgon was possibly one of them since the beheaded corpse "still clasped the Red Arrow" in his hand.
The scouts found signs indicating that the two "Errand-riders of Gondor"
were riding back westwards when they were killed.
Elfhelm deduced that, if they obtained fresh horses from the posts as such riders usually did, the riders reached the Rammas two days earlier, but that they could not pass through it because it had either already been taken by or was already under attack by the forces of Sauron and that the two riders had turned back westwards.
As a result, Théoden concluded that Denethor had not received his message that Rohan was coming and would despair that the Rohirrim would not come to help Minas Tirith.
Etymology
Hirgon
is apparently a
Sindarin
name with an unclear meaning.
It might possibly mean "valiant lord"
from a combination of
hir
("lord, master")
and
-gon
("valor;
hero;
commander").
Alternatively,
Hirgon
might mean "stone lord"
or "lord of Gondor" from
-gon(d)
("stone")
and
hîr
("lord").
In other versions
In the first very rough draft version of the chapter "The Muster of Rohan" the messenger that was sent by Denethor to Théoden is "
Halbarad
sister-son of Denethor" and it is not mentioned that he introduced himself to Théoden or that he is questioned by Théoden if Denethor knew more than he said.
In the fifth draft version, Tolkien initially called the messenger Halbarad, but changed the name to
Barahir
in the margin.
In a later draft labeled "K", the name of the messenger was
Dirgon
.
In a typescript labeled "M" derived from a manuscript labeled "L", the messenger's name was changed to Hirgon.
In this same draft, Hirgon's helm depicts "a small silver crown" rather than a star.
He also mentions "the Harad" instead of "the Haradrim".
In a brief outline of "The Ride of the Rohirrim" chapter, Tolkien states outright that Hirgon is the beheaded corpse.
Inspiration
In
The Lord of the Rings: A Reader's Companion
,
Wayne G. Hammond
and
Christina Scull
explain that Hirgon's role in using the Red Arrow to request aid from Rohan may be influened by
William Morris
's
A Tale of the House of the Wolfings
where a messenger carries "the token of the war-arrow ragged and burnt and bloody" to request the aid of the Wolfings against the
Romans
.
In adaptations
Middle-earth Role Playing
It is mentioned in a
1987
supplement for the
Middle-earth Role Playing
game that Hirgon was a level 14 Dúnedan Scout/Rogue known as the
Stone Lord
who served as Denethor II's chief messenger.
Pán prsteňov
Roman Pomajbo voiced the character in the
2001
-
2003
three-season Slovak radio serial adaptation of
The Lord of the Rings
.
The Lord of the Rings: The Card Game
In
The Lord of the Rings: The Card Game
, Hirgon appears as the fifty-fifth card for
Beneath the Sands
pack.
The card has an threat level of 9 and has an accompanying illustration by Aleksander Karcz.
The Lord of the Rings Online
Hirgon's body in
The Lord of the Rings Online
In the
2007
video game,
The Lord of the Rings Online
, Denethor looked into the Palantir more than a week after Hirgon left Minas Tirith and had come to the believe that Hirgon had never even reached Rohan, and that no aid would thus come to the city.
In the second quest of Book V in Volume IV, the player travels to
Talath Anor
outside the northern wall of the Rammas Echor and finds Hirgon's body alongside the mortally wounded
Andothol
, his companion.

Hirluin
Biographical information
Other names
Hirluin the Fair, Hirluin of the Green Hills
Titles
Lord of
Pinnath Gelin
‎
Birth
Late
Third Age
?
Death
March 15
,
TA 3019
Spouse
Unknown
Weapon
Sword, bow
Physical description
Race
Men
Height
Tall
Culture
Men of Gondor
Hirluin
, known fully as
Hirluin the Fair
or
Hirluin of the Green Hills
, was a
Man of Gondor
, and the Lord of
Pinnath Gelin‎
, who came to the aid of
Minas Tirith
during the
War of the Ring
.
Biography
Hirluin's early history was not known; only that he was lord of the Pinnath Gelin‎ area of
Gondor
. He was the leader of three-hundred gallant green-clad and well-armed men raised for the defence of Gondor, fighting in the
Battle of the Pelennor Fields
.
Hirluin slew many
Orcs
during the siege but he came to his doom when he was overwhelmed by the Orc horde at the breaking of
Minas Tirith
's
Great Gate
. Hirluin was buried before the gate at which he fell alongside
Forlong
. Afterwards, the memory of his death as with others was lamented in the
Mounds of Mundburg
.
Etymology
Hirluin
was a
Sindarin
word meaning 'blue lord'.
Portrayals in adaptations
Hirluin in
The Lord of the Rings Online
In
The Lord of the Rings Online
, Hirluin is first met in
Minas Tirith
shortly before the Battle of the Pelennor Fields, drinking in a tavern and remembering the days of their youth with his good friend
Forlong
. Later, both of them lead the defence of the White City, fighting on the wall alongside other defenders. When the
Rohirrim
army arrives, both of them rush outside the city gates and faces their foes together until Hirluin is slain before his friend.

PAGE: Hob Hayward
CATEGORIES: Hobbits, Minor characters (The Lord of the Rings)
URL: https://lotr.fandom.com/wiki/Hob_Hayward
======================================================================

Hob Hayward
Biographical information
Birth
Late
Third Age
Realms
Shire
Weapon
Sticks
Physical description
Race
Hobbits
Gender
Male
Culture
Shire
-hobbits (
Hayward family
)
Hob Hayward
was a
Hobbit
of the
Shire
.
Biography
Hob Hayward was a two-feathered
Shirriff
. Before the
War of the Ring
, he had watched the
Buckland Gate
as a doorguard. He was well acquainted with the
Brandybuck
family.
During
Lotho Sackville-Baggins
's reign over the Shire, the Shirriffs were reorganized. Hob was old, and one of several shirriffs who were to guard the new gate at the
Brandywine Bridge
under the command of
Bill Ferny
.
When
Frodo Baggins
and his companions returned from their adventure, they found Hob at the gate, who forbade them to pass. After they chased off Ferny, Hob informed them of the changes in their homeland. Although was warned by the other guards, he still told Frodo and his company the truth.
His further fate is unknown, though he possibly joined the
Battle of Bywater
with the other Shirriffs that switched sides.
Etymology
A
hayward
is a local official with the duty of inspecting fences and keeping cattle from straying.
In adaptations
In
The Lord of the Rings Online
, Shirriff Hob Hayward, found at the Gate of Buckland, asks the player to investigate a disturbance within the
Old Forest
.
Trivia
In the
Chinese
translation, Hayward is translated as "海妖", which means "Sea Monster".
See also
The Scouring of the Shire
Sharkey
Translations
Foreign Language
Translated name
Chinese (Hong Kong)
霍伯·海妖
French
Hob Gardeclôture
German
Hugo Feldhüter
Italian
Hob Guardasiepe

======================================================================
PAGE: Horn
CATEGORIES: Minor characters (The Lord of the Rings)
URL: https://lotr.fandom.com/wiki/Horn
======================================================================

This article refers to the
Rider of
Rohan
. For other namesakes, see
Horn (disambiguation)
.
Horn
Biographical information
Titles
King's Rider
Birth
Possibly late
Third Age
Death
March 15
,
3019
Weapon
Sword
Physical description
Race
Men
Gender
Male
Culture
Rohirrim
Horn
was a
Man of Rohan
who died in the
Battle of the Pelennor Fields
.
Afterwards, the memory of his death as with others is lamented in the
Song of the Mounds of Mundburg
.
In adaptations
In
The Lord of the Rings Online
, Horn is a major character, who accompanies the player throughout Riddermark.
He comes from the town of Eaworth in the
Entwash
Vale is the youngest child of its Reeve Ingbert. He has an older brother Ingmar and a sister Ingyth. Though capable with a sword, Horn wished in his heart to be a minstrel, of which his father did not approve. He had apprenticeship under King
Théoden
's minster
Gléowine
until he spoke out against
Gríma Wormtongue
and was sentenced to the border outpost of Stangard, north of the river
Limlight
, and forbidden to set foot into
Rohan
proper.
It is in Stangard where the player first meets Horn, accompanied by a young woman from
Dunland
, Nona, who had been waging her own private war against
Saruman
after her brother was killed by his servants. The Rohirrim men do not allow the
Dunlending
into their settlement, but otherwise raise no arms against her. Nona and the player then go on an expedition into the
Brown Lands
across the Great River, and on their way back Nona is wounded in an
Easterling
attack. The men of Stangard refuse to aid her with the sole exception of Horn, who helps the player take Nona into the
Golden Wood
to the north, where Nona's life is saved by Lady
Galadriel
. Though he barely set wood into the forest, the other men of Rohan feel that Horn has been affected by it and he is no longer welcome in Stangard. He then joins the company of the player, Nona and a
Galadhrim
Elf
named Corudan in their journey across Rohan in the defiance of the ban that had been placed upon him.
Having acquired steeds, the group comes across the signs of a passing band of
Uruk-hai
that captured
Merry
and
Pippin
and follows them across the plain on horseback. On their journey Horn and Nona begin developing affection towards one another, which comes to test when the group reaches Horn's hometown of Eaworth. Horn's father is angry at him for abandoning his post, but none of the Rohirrim wishes to as much see a Dunlending walking among them. Nona angrily storms out and unable to find her, Horn, Corudan and the player continue on until they meet
Éomer
and his riders. They travel them to the town of
Snowbourn
and then to
Edoras
, where Gríma orders Éomer imprisoned and the rest banished from returning under penalty of death. King's minstrel and Horn's mentor
Gléowine
is exiles along with them and the group to investigate the situation in Gléowine's home country of Wildermore, from which no word has come out in some weeks. The group finds Wildermore ravaged by Saruman's forces and meets Nona, who had been helping the survivors, which mellowed her attitude towards the Rohirrim. Horn and Nona reconcile their feelings for each other and the group decides that despite the penalty of death the King must hear of what has transpired. Nona and Corudan the Elf stay behind while the player, Norn and Gléowine return to Edoras, arriving soon after Théoden has been healed by
Gandalf
.
Gléowine joins the King's side as he rides to war, while Éomer asks the player and Horn to check on his home town of
Aldburg
. There they reunite with Nona and Corudan and returns to Edoras, where Lady
Éowyn
makes the last preparations before leading the people to
Dunharrow
. An agent of Wormtongue is discovered but flees the city and the player, Horn, Nona and Corudan chase after him across
Westemnet
. In the province of Stonedeans ruled by Reeve
Herubrand
they receive the word of a large raiding party of Dunlendings loyal to Saruman that had come from the mountains. Nona wishes to bring vengeance on those of her people who sided with the
wizard
and Horn follows her while the player and Corudan turn south towards
Helm's Deep
. The player does not encounter Horn again until much later, when Théoden's army makes its way through the
Drúadan Forest
. Horn reveals that Nona did not in truth seek the raiding the party, but wanted to escape the war now that she is pregnant with his child. She begged Horn to come with her, but he could not abandon his duty and answered the King's muster of the Rohirrim, knowing that he would never see Nona again one way or another.
Horn's ultimate fate depends on a decision made by the player much earlier in the game. During the summoning of the
Grey Company
,
Ranger
Halros guarding the borders of the
Shire
can be persuaded to either journey south to the aid of
Aragorn
or stay and perform the very task that Aragorn, his
Chieftain
, personally instructed him to do. If Halros stays in the Shire, Horn dies in the
Battle of the Pelennor Fields
. However, if Halros is persuaded to go south, he shields Horn from the mortal blow at the cost of his own life. Horrified by the death around him and the sacrifice of a man he didn't even know, Horn loses his will to go on and abandons his duty. He forsakes his name and, now a broken man, leaves into paths unknown, telling the player to report him as dead.
References

Hugo Bracegirdle

(father)

)

was a
Hobbit
of the
Shire
.
Biography
Hugo was the son of
Bruno Bracegirdle
and was born in
SR 1350
.
He attended the
Farewell Party
of
Bilbo Baggins
in
TA 3001
, and afterwards received a book-case from Bilbo as a present and a joke; for Hugo borrowed many books during his life, both from Bilbo and others, and was very poor at returning them.

PAGE: Háma
CATEGORIES: Minor characters (The Lord of the Rings)
URL: https://lotr.fandom.com/wiki/Háma

This article is about the
doorward of
Meduseld
. For the son of
Helm Hammerhand
, see
Háma
.
Háma
Biographical information
Titles
Doorward of Meduseld, Captain of the King's guard
Death
March 3
or
4
,
3019
Physical description
Race
Men
Media portrayal
Actor
John Leigh
Voice
Tom Kane
(
video game
)
"
I believe you are friends and folk worthy of honour, who have no evil purpose. You may go in.
"
—
Háma to
Gandalf
in
The Two Towers
Háma
was a
Man of Rohan
, the doorward of
Meduseld
and captain of
Théoden
's guard.
Biography
Háma greeted
Gandalf
,
Aragorn
,
Legolas
and
Gimli
when they arrived to speak with King Théoden. He told the company to leave their weapons behind, by order of
Gríma Wormtongue
, before entering the King's hall. He disarmed Aragorn, Legolas, and Gimli, but against the King's orders permitted Gandalf to keep his staff with him as a courtesy, though he knew, as he remarked, that "The staff in the hand of a wizard may be more than a prop for age."
Háma was present at the
Battle of the Hornburg
, and died defending the gates of the
Hornburg
. He was buried at Helm's Deep after the battle.
In adaptations
Peter Jackson's
The Two Towers
In the
The Lord of the Rings: The Two Towers
, Háma was still the doorward of Théoden. He allows Gandalf to pass with his staff as well. He is later killed by a
Warg
-rider scout during Saruman's attack on the retreating Rohirrim, and then devoured by the Warg while its rider fights
Gamling
and is killed by
Legolas
. Háma's body is left behind. His only known relation is his son,
Haleth
(a non-canon character), who is one of the many youth made to protect Helm's Deep.
New Zealand
actor
John Leigh
plays Háma.
Voice dubbing actors
Foreign Language
Voice dubbing artist
German
Hans-Georg Panczak
Radio
Michael Spice
provided the voice for Háma in
The Lord of the Rings
1981 radio series.
Video games
In
The Lord of the Rings Online
, Háma escorts the player and their companions to an audience with King
Théoden
on two separate occasions. He later travels from
Edoras
to
Helm's Deep
, where after the battle a song of his valiant last stand before the gates is sung by King's minstrel
Gléowine
.
Háma makes an appearance in
LEGO The Lord of the Rings: The Video Game
, where he is a playable character, and can be purchased at the gate of Edoras.

The Lord of the Rings Online

The Lord of the Rings: The Card Game
,
The Long Dark Adventure Pack

,

Middle-earth Strategy Battle Game

======================================================================
PAGE: Húrin (Warden of the Keys)
CATEGORIES: Men of Gondor, Minor characters (The Lord of the Rings)
URL: https://lotr.fandom.com/wiki/Húrin_(Warden_of_the_Keys)
======================================================================

"Can you see anything?" "Nothing. There's nothing."
The descriptive majority of this article's text is
unsourced
, and should be supported with
references
.
This article refers to the
man of Gondor
. For other namesakes, see
Húrin (disambiguation)
.
Húrin in
The Lord of the Rings Online
Húrin
(sometimes named
Húrin the Tall
) was a man of
Gondor
during the events of the
War of the Ring
. He lived in
Minas Tirith
and there he held the title of
Warden of the Keys
. He was a high ranking official of Gondor. For a short while he also ruled the city when
Faramir
was in the
Houses of Healing
and
Imrahil
had ridden out to the
Black Gate
of
Mordor
with
Aragorn
and the Army of the West.
Biography
War of the Ring
Húrin was first mentioned during the events of
The Return of the King
. As an official of Gondor, he assisted
Gandalf
and
Imrahil
in directing the defence of Minas Tirith when the forces of
Mordor
besieged it.
Later, once
Théoden
and the men of
Rohan
had arrived, Húrin joined Imrahil,
Hirluin
and
Forlong
in riding out on horse with many other men to aid the
Rohirrim
in the
Battle of the Pelennor Fields
. Although many, including Théoden, Hirluin and Forlong would fall in the battle, Húrin was amongst the survivors.
Later, when Aragorn and Imrahil led the army of the west to challenge
Sauron
at the Black Gate, Húrin temporarily took on the role of ruler of the city, as Faramir was recovering from his encounter with the
Witch-king
. Húrin was left with a substantial garrison of men in order to defend it should Sauron decided to bring more
Haradrim
up out of the south and assault it while the other captains rode towards Mordor. Húrin was aided in his defence of the city by
Elfhelm
, a
Marshal
of Rohan and some two thousand
Rohirrim
. Their numbers were further bolstered by a great number of men that arrived out of Gondor's southern lands, led by
Angbor
the Fearless, who was the Lord of
Lamedon
. Due to this, the city under Húrin's stewardship was better manned than it had been before the
Siege of Gondor
had started.
After the War
By the time the army of the west returned in victory to Minas Tirith, Faramir had returned to health and had assumed the office of
Steward
. Hurin went with Faramir to greet the returning army, and was one of the major witnesses to the crowning of Aragorn, the new
King of Gondor
. In his role as Warden of the Keys, it was Húrin who opened up the temporary barrier that was placed in front of the broken gate of Minas Tirith in order allow Aragorn and the rest of the army to enter the city.
Nothing else is known of Húrin after this point, though presumably he retained his position within the city after Aragorn became king.
[
citation needed
]
In adaptations
Video games
In
The Lord of the Rings Online
, before the siege of
Minas Tirith
begins, Húrin can be found right inside the city gates, making the final preparations for the upcoming battle. During the siege proper he fights on the walls of the First Circle alongside other Lords of
Gondor
. After the battle is won, he is found right outside the city gates next to
Imrahil
and tasks the player with quenching the remaining fires and burying those who died fighting within the First Circle. He stands behind Steward Faramir during the coronation of King Elessar.
Tabletop games
Húrin the Tall is featured in
Middle-earth Strategy Battle Game
, introduced in the 2019 supplement
Gondor at War
.
Gallery
Hurin as seen in
Middle-earth Strategy Battle Game

======================================================================
PAGE: Ingold
CATEGORIES: Men of Gondor, Minor characters (The Lord of the Rings)
URL: https://lotr.fandom.com/wiki/Ingold
======================================================================

Ingold
Biographical information
Birth
Late
Third Age
Physical description
Race
Men
Gender
Male
Culture
Men of Gondor
"
We wish for no strangers in the land at this time, unless they be mighty men of arms in whose faith and help we can trust.
"
—
The Return of the King
, "
Minas Tirith
"
Ingold
was a
Man of Gondor
and a soldier of the realm.
Biography
During the
War of the Ring
, he was the leader of the soldiers guarding Forannest, the northern gate of the
Rammas Echor
as it was being repaired just before the start of the
Siege of Gondor
. He permitted
Gandalf
and
Pippin
through the gate, having recognized the
wizard
immediately, but he enquired after Pippin's business in
Minas Tirith
as Gondorians of the time did not trust foreigners and
Halflings
were almost completely unknown to him, aside from the one mentioned in
Boromir
's dream.
Days after Gandalf had passed, an army out of
Mordor
crossed the river
Anduin
at
Cair Andros
and descended on Ingold's position. He retreated with those of his men who survived back to the city, reporting that the northern road had fallen into the hands of the enemy. This was woeful news in Minas Tirith, because it was thought to mean that the
Rohirrim
could not come to their aid (though in fact the Riders were able to find another route, the long-forgotten
Stonewain Valley
).
Etymology
The name
Ingold
likely had close etymological ties with the word
Ingoldo
, meaning 'the
Ñoldor
' in
Quenya
.
Other versions of the legendarium
In earlier writings, Ingold was the name of Aragorn and the
Elfstone
.
In adaptations
Ingold in
The Lord of the Rings Online
Video games
In
The Lord of the Rings Online
Ingold is first met defending the northern gate of
Rammas Echor
when the wall is already besieged by the Morgul host. He tasks the player with slowing the enemy's advance and repelling the attacks on the wall, but eventually retreats to
Minas Tirith
along with his men. Ingold survives the
Battle of the Pelennor Fields
and joins the
Host of the West
on the march to the
Black Gate
. After the downfall of
Sauron
he becomes part of
Gondorian
army tasked with entering the Black Land and dealing with the evil that remains within. Ingold and his men establish a camp in the ruins of Díngarth, an ancient Gondorian keep on the plain of Dol Amarth, from which they make several incursions to the ruins of
Barad-dûr
, which are found to be neither empty nor free of danger. After King
Elessar
charges
Steward
Faramir
with cleansing the evil of
Minas Morgul
, Ingold and his men traverse the plateau of
Gorgoroth
and enter the
Morgul Vale
from the east.
Tabletop games
Ingold is featured in
Middle-earth Strategy Battle Game
, introduced in the 2019 supplement
Gondor at War
.
Gallery
Ingold as seen in
Middle-earth Strategy Battle Game
Ingold in
The Lord of the Rings: The Card Game
,
The Wastes of Eriador Adventure Pack
Ingold's miniature

PAGE: Ioreth
CATEGORIES: Men of Gondor, Minor characters (The Lord of the Rings)
URL: https://lotr.fandom.com/wiki/Ioreth
======================================================================

This article is about the
healer of
Minas Tirith
. For the wife of the Ranger
Talion
, see
Ioreth
.
Ioreth
Biographical information
Titles
Healer of the
Houses of Healing
Birth
Late
Third Age
?
Death
Unknown
Physical description
Race
Men
Gender
Female
Culture
Men of Gondor
Ioreth
was a woman of
Gondor
, originally from the province of
Lossarnach
and a healer.
Biography
Ioreth was born in
Imloth Melui
sometime in the late
Third Age
. At the time of the
War of the Ring
, Ioreth was the eldest of the women who served in the
Houses of Healing
in
Minas Tirith
. Her folk wisdom was exceeded only by her loquacity. She was the one who told
Gandalf
the old lore that "The hands of the king are the hands of a healer," which inspired him to bring
Aragorn
to the Houses of Healing to tend the wounded. She had at least two sisters and kin who lived in Imloth Melui.
In adaptations
Ioreth in
The Lord of the Rings Online
Radio
Ioreth in
The Lord of the Rings: The Card Game
, A Storm on Cobas Haven Adventure Pack
Prunella Margaret Rumney West Scales
voiced her in the second run fifth episode of "Minas Tirith and Mount Doom" in the
The Lord of the Rings
(1956 radio series)
Pat Franklyn
voiced a woman who apparently was Ioreth in the
The Lord of the Rings
(1979 radio series)
Pauline Letts
voiced Ioreth in
The Lord of the Rings
(1981 radio series)
.
Vilma Jamnická voiced the character in the 2001-2003 three-season Slovak radio serial adaptation of
The Lord of the Rings
.
Video games
In
The Lord of the Rings Online
, Ioreth is found in the Houses of Healing after the battle, where she asks the player to assist her with mixing herbs and delivering medicine to the wounded.

======================================================================
PAGE: Isengarders
CATEGORIES: Agents of Saruman
URL: https://lotr.fandom.com/wiki/Isengarders
======================================================================

Isengarders
Origins
Orcs
produced through "special breeding" possibly from some
Northern Orcs
of the
Misty Mountains
,
Orcs cross-bred with
Men
, or Men turned into Uruks or Orcs
Examples
Uglúk
,
Mauhúr
Dominions
Isengard
Languages
Orkish
(
Black Speech
),
Westron
Divisions
Half-orcs
,
Snaga
,
Uruk-hai
,
Wolf-riders
Lifespan
Unknown
"
Curse the Isengarders! Uglúk u bagronk sha pushdug Saruman-glob búb-hosh skai.
"
—
A
Mordor-orc
in the mixed company of Orcs taking
Merry
and
Pippin
from
Amon Hen
"
Isengarders
"
was a collective term for the various breeds of
Orcs
that emerged from experiments in
Isengard
. They served the fallen
Wizard
Saruman
, treacherous vassal of the
Dark Lord
Sauron
, in the late
Third Age
. The term was used particularly for the
Uruk-hai
of the
White Hand
more than for other Orcs.
History
Coveting the
One Ring
,
Saruman
secretly betrayed the
White Council
and began building an
Orc
army
at Isengard. Around the year
2990
of the
Third Age
, he drew some of the
Northern Orcs
of the
Misty Mountains
and powerful
Uruk-hai
.
into his service, keeping them in pits of mud carved out beneath Isengard.
It is said that Saruman may have used these Orcs for purposes of "special breeding"
in many experiments. Some of these experiments were influenced by the methods
of
Morgoth
and
Sauron
and involved cross-breeding Orcs with
Men
,
creating many different breeds of hybrids such as the vile and cunning
Half-orcs
and powerful Orc-men.
Others (as noted by Treebeard) may have been "Men [Saruman had] ruined. . ."(The Two Towers), presumably of Dunland or Rohirric captives or outcasts, who were altered by Saruman to be the forefathers (or foremothers) of the Isengarders and Isengard Uruk-hai in particular.
By the year
3000
, having become "an ally, or servant of Sauron",
Saruman sent some of the Isengarders (many being "great Uruks") to join the
Mordor-orcs
and
Moria-orcs
in raids upon the eastern villages of
Rohan
.
By the time he took
Gandalf
captive in Isengard, the number of Isengarders produced from Saruman's experiments had grown, now including Orc-like creatures he used as
Wolf-riders
.
The Isengarders had defiled
Nan Curunír
, turning the valley into a "slave's flattery" of
Mordor
,
filled with forges, furnaces, and other machines.
The Orcs procured their wood from
Fangorn Forest
, earning the enmity of the
Ents
.
Isengarder with White Hand shield, as depicted by
John Howe
At the start of the
War of the Ring
, the Isengarders began displaying the
White Hand
on their banners, faces, helms, and shields.
In the year
3019
, a large army of Isengarders and
Dunlendings
engaged the
Rohirrim
forces in the
First Battle of the Fords of Isen
, dispatched with orders to kill
Théoden
's heir; an
Orc-man
mortally-wounded
Théodred
, after which Saruman's minions retreated.
Saruman also sent a
horde
of Uruk-hai led by
Uglúk
to attack the
Fellowship of the Ring
at
Amon Hen
and attain the
One Ring
.
Though the Isengarders captured two
Hobbits
and slew
Boromir
,
their return journey was slowed by conflict with some
Moria-orcs
and the Mordor-orcs sent by Sauron himself.
All of the Orcs were slain in an attack by
Éomer
and his
company
on the eaves of Fangorn Forest.
Meanwhile,  Isengarders and Dunlendings wreaked havoc in the
Westfold
of Rohan. Caught as traitor to both the
Free Peoples of the World
and his new master, Saruman tried to appease Sauron by sending his 10,000 strong army  to crush the people of Rohan at
Helm's Deep
. The Orcs and the Wild Men were vanquished in the
Battle of the Hornburg
.
The remaining Isengarders were destroyed alongside Saruman's other servants in the
last march of the Ents
.
In adaptations
In the film tie-in
The Lord of the Rings: Weapons and Warfare
, the Northern Orcs recruited by Saruman are referred to as
Saruman's Orcs
. Instructed by his new master to build him a second army, it is said that Saruman replicated Sauron's methods of breeding Uruk-hai by crossing Orcs with Goblin-men.
The Toybiz toy-line uses the term
Isengard Orc
.
Saruman's Orcs, as seen in
The Lord of the Rings film trilogy
In the film,
The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring
, Saruman's Orcs are called to Isengard to begin the final preparations for war. Carrying out "orders from Mordor", they cut down the tree gardens around the
Tower of Orthanc
to reveal a network of caverns filled with forges, furnaces, and other machines. Inside the pits, Saruman's Orcs made up arms for their masters' forces and dug out the Uruk-hai from their birth-pods.
In the film,
The Lord of the Rings: The Two Towers
, Saruman instructs an Orc overseer to hasten with the completion of his army; he sanctions the burning of Fangorn wood to fuel his war machine. At his master's behest, the White Wizard begins sending raiding parties of his Orcs and Dunlendings to terrorize the Westfold of Rohan. Saruman's Orcs are destroyed during the Ents' attack on Isengard, being either crushed to death or drowned after the breaking of the dam.
In other versions
In the index of
The Lord of the Rings
which was not made by
J.R.R. Tolkien
, the term "Isengarders" is listed as being an alternative name of the Uruk-hai despite Tolkien's use of the term including the Orcs and Half-orcs of Isengard as well.
References

======================================================================
PAGE: King of the Dead
CATEGORIES: Minor characters (The Lord of the Rings)
URL: https://lotr.fandom.com/wiki/King_of_the_Dead
======================================================================

King of the Dead
Biographical information
Titles
King of the Mountains
Birth
Second Age
Death
Sometime after the
War of the Last Alliance
(as a Man),
March 13
, 3019 (final death)
Realms
White Mountains
,
Paths of the Dead
Weapon
Spear
Physical description
Race
Men
, later
Undead
Culture
Dead Men of Dunharrow
Media portrayal
Actor
Paul Norell
Voice
Steve Blum
(
video game
)
"
... all that were left fled to the hills at the rumour of the coming of the King of the Dead.
"
—
The Return of the King
, "
The Passing of the Grey Company
"
The
King of the Dead
, originally "
King of the Mountains
", was the king of the
Dead Men of Dunharrow
in
Dwimorberg
, and before that had been a living
man
. His spirit resided along with those of his kin in the
Paths of the Dead
.
Biography
In the later years of the
Second Age
, he and his men also served and worshiped
Sauron
, until he and his people swore allegiance to the realm of
Gondor
upon the
Stone of Erech
.
Isildur
was the
King of Gondor
at that time. The King of the Dead made an oath to fight on Isildur's side when needed, but when that moment of need arrived in the
War of the Last Alliance
, he did not come. Isildur cursed the King of the Dead and his people, and as a result, the King of the Dead and his people could not rest until their oath was fulfilled.
The King of the Dead in the films, portrayed by
Paul Norell
In the late
Third Age
, the king and his people joined with
Aragorn
and the
Grey Company
during the
War of the Ring
, after Aragorn passed through Dwimorberg and summoned them at the
Stone of Erech
.
The
Dead Men of Dunharrow
then followed Aragorn to
Pelargir
, where they defeated the
Corsairs of Umbar
. Aragorn, as Heir of Isildur, subsequently declared the oath of the Dead Men as fulfilled. Upon hearing this, the king broke his spear and threw it aside then bowed to Aragorn, turned away, and with the rest of his people "vanished like a mist that is driven back by a sudden wind," never to be seen again.
In adaptations
The Lord of the Rings
film trilogy
The way is...shut. It was made by those who are dead...and the dead keep it. The way is shut.
—The King of the Dead
In
The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King
, several details are changed from the book. This king first appears to Aragorn as a quick vision when he is looking at the
Dimholt
road. He later manifests to Aragorn,
Legolas
, and
Gimli
when they are taking the
Paths of the Dead
. He spars briefly with Aragorn, resisting Aragorn's authority at first but later agreeing to fight. The king and his army attack the Corsairs, preventing them from entering Gondor, after which they accompany Aragorn, Legolas, and Gimli to
Minas Tirith
. To complete the oath he helped defend Minas Tirith when it was under attack by an army of
Orcs
and
Trolls
and their leader the
Witch-king of Angmar
. After the
battle
the curse was broken by Aragorn, putting the Oathbreakers at rest.
Voice dubbing actors
Foreign Language
Voice dubbing artist
Spanish (Latin America)
Gonzalo Curiel
Spanish (Spain)
Gonzalo Dúran
Portuguese (Brazil) (Television/DVD)
Wellington Lima
German
Jürgen Jung
Czech
Bedřich Šetena
Italian (Italy)
Nino Prester
French (France)
François Siener
Video games
In the console versions of
The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King
video game (for instance, the PlayStation 2 version), he is the first boss on Aragorn, Legolas, and Gimli's path. Other than wielding a large
sword
, he uses sorcery to create blasts of wind called "Wind of the Mountain", and chants spells to summon his undead army including undead archers and swordsmen. If one listens closely, it appears and sounds as if he is reciting part of the first line of the inscription on the
One Ring
, specifically "Ash nazg durbatulûk"
.
He was voiced by
Jairon Monroe
.
In the Game Boy Advance version, he is again a boss fought in the Paths of the Dead and battles Aragorn, Legolas and Gimli under similar circumstances as the console version. Differences from the console version are that his Wind of the Mountain power is limited though his undead soldiers are stronger, and he is confronted by all three of the companions at once whereas only the player character faces him in the console version. Later he appears at Pellenor Fields emerging from the ships after the player has slain the orc commander at the port and orders his army to charge.
In the console versions of
The Lord of the Rings: The Third Age
, he appears briefly during the final skirmishes of the game at Pelennor Fields wherein Aragorn has temporarily joined the player's party and is able to direct the army of the dead to charge into all enemies in the party's way. This is one of the strongest attacks in the game, though only made available to the player for a short amount of time.
The King of the Dead appears as a non-playable character in
The Lord of the Rings: Aragorn's Quest
. In the Wii and PS3 versions, he appears in the penultimate story level and is fought as an optional boss in the Shire overworld, after that level is completed.  This optional boss fight is the culmination of a particular Side Quest where
Frodo Gamgee
goes into Mushroom Woods to re-enact Aragorn's journey into the Path of the Dead to recruit the Armies of the Dead, fighting him and his men along the way to convince them to join him to save Minas Tirith.
In
The Lord of the Rings Online
, the King of the Dead is named
Rioc
. Although his men were unwilling to fight Sauron because they had worshiped him, his excuse for not honoring the oath given to Isildur was because his daughter Bravantel had an out-of-wedlock child with one of his
Númenórean
warriors. He leads his people after joining Aragorn's host and is freed after the liberation of Pelargir.
In submod of
The Third Age: Total War
(a
Medieval II Total War
modification) called
DCI: Last Alliance
, the King of the Dead is named
Zarazir
. His faction, the Men of the Mountains, can either join an alliance with Islidur's or Sauron's factions, or can be independent and conquer both the Númenóreans and
Mordor
.
In
LEGO The Lord of the Rings: The Video Game
, the King of the Dead is a boss encountered in the Paths of the Dead story mission.
Gallery
The King of the Dead in
The Lord of the Rings: The Card Game
,
The Stone of Erech Standalone Scenario

.

; he and
Muzgash
were killed by
Gorbag
's Orcs in the battle over
Frodo
's
mithril-shirt
.

PAGE: Legolas
CATEGORIES: Elves, Grey Elves, Major characters (The Lord of the Rings), The Lord of the Rings characters
